operating_state: Revenue reached $18.12 billion in Q3 FY2024, up 206% year-over-year and 34% sequentially, driven by Data Center growth from exceptional AI demand across cloud service providers, enterprises, and sovereign AI initiatives. Compute GPU growth and networking revenue increases from InfiniBand infrastructure reflected unprecedented datacenter demand with visibility extending into next year. Gaming revenue grew from normalized channel inventory and strong GeForce RTX demand, while customer concentration remains high with two customers representing approximately 15% and 13% of total revenue respectively.
cost_structure: Gross margin expanded to 74.0% from 53.6% year-over-year, driven by favorable product mix from higher-margin datacenter products comprising larger revenue portion and lower net inventory provisions. Operating expenses increased from compensation increases, stock-based compensation, and expanded go-to-market capabilities for AI solutions. Supply chain complexity increased through expanded purchase volumes, new supplier integration, and prepaid capacity agreements with extended lead times requiring premium payments and deposits.
material_events: USG announced October 17 export controls requiring immediate licenses for advanced GPU exports to China and specified regions, effective October 23 without originally planned delay. Board approved additional $25 billion share repurchase authorization, with $3.72 billion returned through repurchases during Q3. Company launched NVIDIA HGX H200, introduced AI foundry service on Microsoft Azure, and began shipping GH200 Grace Hopper Superchips while transitioning testing and distribution operations out of China and Hong Kong.
macro_exposures: Export licensing requirements restrict products exceeding performance thresholds designed for datacenters, with sales to China historically contributing significant Data Center revenue expected to decline significantly in Q4. Geopolitical tensions affecting Taiwan supply chain pose material risk given manufacturing concentration, while Israeli operations supporting employees experienced military call-ups amid regional conflict.
forward_outlook: Management expects supply increases each quarter through next year with continued supplier and capacity arrangements to meet extended demand visibility from AI infrastructure growth. Broader Data Center product launch cadence planned to address diverse AI opportunities, while company develops regulation-compliant product portfolio including solutions not requiring advance USG notification. Strong global AI demand continues with export licensing requirements not anticipated to have near-term meaningful financial impact as growth in other regions offsets restricted markets.
uncertainty_profile: USG export control rules subject to change at any time with potential expansion of geographic, customer, or product scope, while license approval process lacks clear standards. Supply chain disruption risk from Taiwan restrictions or natural disasters affecting concentrated manufacturing base, combined with inventory provision risk if prepaid commitments exceed demand. Product transition challenges magnified by shorter development cycles may impact supply-demand management.
